text: Saguaro National Park
zh: 萨瓜罗国家公园

text: A monarch. The Saguaro,
zh: 一位君王。萨瓜罗巨柱仙人掌，

text: king of all cactuses, and the royal icon of the American Southwest.
zh: 仙人掌之王，美国西南部的皇家象征。

text: Here in the Sonoran Desert is the only place on earth where the Saguaro grows.
zh: 在这片索诺兰沙漠中，是地球上唯一能生长萨瓜罗仙人掌的地方。

text: The home of Saguaro National Park, near the Arizona -Mexican border,
zh: 萨瓜罗国家公园坐落于亚利桑那与墨西哥边境附近，

text: there are 91 ,000 protected acres bisected by the city of Tucson.
zh: 占地9.1万英亩，被图森市一分为二。

text: In this park, the saguaro is a thirsty monarch, sucking up as much as 200
zh: 在这座公园里，萨瓜罗就像一位口渴的君王，在罕见但倾盆而下的沙漠暴雨中，能一次吸收多达200

text: gallons of water during a rare but torrential desert rainstorm.
zh: 加仑的水。

text: These royal giants grow slowly, but can reach 50 feet in height
zh: 这些庞然大物生长缓慢，却能长到50英尺高，

text: and reign through a lifespan of 150 years.
zh: 寿命长达150年，堪称统治沙漠的君主。

text: Solid as they appear to be, it is water that makes up most of their weight.
zh: 尽管看起来坚实厚重，但它们大部分的重量其实都来自水分。

text: They are the most recognized cactus in the world.
zh: 它们是世界上最具辨识度的仙人掌。

text: The saguaro has become a symbol of the American West.
zh: 萨瓜罗已成为美国西部的象征。

text: Park visitors head out on the Cactus Forest Trail, a desert thoroughfare much used by hikers and bicycles.
zh: 游客们常沿着仙人掌森林步道前行，这是徒步者和骑行者都爱走的沙漠通道。

text: Hikers must avoid brushing against the spines of the low -growing, shovel -like Cholla cactus.
zh: 徒步者必须小心，避免碰到低矮如铲子般的丛柱仙人掌的刺。

text: They bristle with barbed spines, which are painfully difficult to remove once embedded in your flesh.
zh: 这些刺带倒钩，一旦扎进皮肉，拔除时会异常疼痛。

text: Many desert plants bristle. The agave.
zh: 许多沙漠植物都长满尖刺，比如龙舌兰。

text: Water is so scarce that cactuses protect the water they have stored with sharp spines.
zh: 由于水分极其稀缺，仙人掌用锋利的刺来保护体内储存的水分。

text: The prickly pear. Without these spines, insects, birds, and animals would eat them.
zh: 还有仙人梨。若没有这些刺，昆虫、鸟类和动物早就把它们吃光了。

text: At first glance, this expansive desert seems lifeless.
zh: 乍一看，这片广袤的沙漠似乎毫无生机。

text: Too much sun. Too little rain.
zh: 阳光过于强烈，雨水过于稀少。

text: But a longer look reveals a profusion of wildlife.
zh: 但仔细观察，就会发现这里其实生机勃勃。

text: constant scarcity of water, each creature struggles endlessly to stay alive. Water is life.
zh: 面对持续的水源匮乏，每种生物都在不断挣扎求生。水，就是生命。

text: The wriggle saguaro provides sweet fruit for hungry creatures and homes to birds,
zh: 高大的萨瓜罗为饥饿的动物提供甜美的果实，也为鸟类提供栖身之所，

text: such as the Harris Hawk and the elf owl, the smallest owl in existence.
zh: 比如哈里斯鹰，以及世界上最小的猫头鹰——精灵鸮。

text: Like all deserts, Saguaro has extreme fluctuations in daily temperatures.
zh: 和所有沙漠一样，萨瓜罗国家公园的昼夜温差极大。

text: Cloudless skies and unshaded ground make a blast furnace of the daytime.
zh: 万里无云、毫无遮蔽的地面让白天酷热如熔炉。

text: While at night, without cloud cover or moisture to hold the heat, bitter cold takes over.
zh: 而到了夜晚，没有云层或水汽保温，严寒便随之而来。

text: Some creatures burrow underground or crawl into rock dens to escape the killer sun of summer and the cold nights of winter.
zh: 一些生物会钻入地下或藏进岩穴，以躲避夏季的酷暑和冬季的严寒。

text: When summer temperatures soar to over 100 degrees Fahrenheit,
zh: 当夏季气温飙升至华氏100度以上时，

text: scorpions exude a coating of wax to seal in moisture, an effective barrier against dehydration.
zh: 蝎子会分泌一层蜡质，锁住体内水分，有效防止脱水。

text: The only known year -round spring is found at King Canyon in the Tucson Mountains.
zh: 图森山脉中的金峡谷，是已知唯一全年不断流的泉水所在地。

text: This reliable source of water is often frequented by the collared peccary,
zh: 这处可靠的水源常有西貒光顾，

text: a harried wild mammal. They're also known by their Spanish name, javelina.
zh: 它们是一种忙碌的野生哺乳动物，也常被称为“javelina”（西班牙语名称）。

text: When not sleeping during the heat of day, these members of the New World pig family are found rooting among beds of prickly
zh: 这些新大陆猪科动物白天炎热时多在休息，其余时间则常在仙人梨丛中觅食

text: pear. They're social animals, usually traveling in herds.
zh: 它们是群居动物，通常成群结队地活动。

text: The king of cactus and profuse wildlife aren't the park's only attractions.
zh: 仙人掌之王与繁盛的野生动物并非公园唯一的看点。

text: Red Hills Visitor Center in the Tucson Mountain District offers an insight to the Native American take on the saguaro cactus.
zh: 图森山区的红山游客中心展示了美洲原住民对萨瓜罗仙人掌的独特理解。

text: Signal Hill is well worth the hike. Here, some thousand years ago,
zh: 信号山值得一走。大约一千年前，

text: near what is today an inviting picnic area, Native Americans left hundreds of petroglyphs.
zh: 就在如今这片宜人的野餐区附近，美洲原住民留下了数百幅岩画。

text: Though we have never deciphered the meaning of these pictorial records, we feel certain that the prehistoric people who made them
zh: 尽管我们从未破译这些图画所记录的含义，但可以肯定，创作它们的史前先民

text: hiked in these forests of the Saguaro and groves of Mesquite, probing the secrets of the desert as we do today.
zh: 曾走过这片萨瓜罗森林和牧豆树林，探索沙漠的奥秘，正如我们今天所做的那样。

text: Thus, this seemingly dead earth reveals itself to be a land of life.
zh: 于是，这片看似毫无生机的土地，展现出它作为生命之地的真实面貌。
